title: Mirror Formula, Reflection in Spherical Mirror

concepts.title: Reflection from Spherical Mirror
concepts.conceptBody:
Reflection from Spherical Mirror

Some important definitions : 

1.Spherical mirrors : A spherical mirror is a part of follow sphere or a spherical surface. They are classified as concave or convex according to the reflecting surface being concave or convex respectively.

 

2.Centre of curvature : The center $\text{C}$ of the sphere of which mirror is a part is called the center of curvature of the mirror.

3.Pole or Vertex : $\text{P}$, the center of the mirror surface is the pole.

4.Radius of curvature (R) : The distance CP is called the radius of curvature.

Note : All distances are measured from point $P$. We can see using the rules of sign convention given earlier that $\text{R}$ is positive for convex mirror and negative for concave mirror.

5.Principal axis : The line CP joining the pole and the center of curvature of the spherical mirror is called the principal axis. 

 

6.Focus (F) : If a parallel beam of rays, parallel to the principal axis and close to it, is incident on a spherical mirror; the reflected rays converge to a point $\text{F}$ (in case of a concave mirror) or appear to diverge from a point $\text{F}$ (in case of convex mirror) on the principal axis. The point $\text{F}$ is called the flows of the spherical mirror.

7.Focal length (F) : Focal length is the distance PF between the pole $\text{P}$ and flows $\text{F}$ along the principal axis.

8.Aperture : The line joining the end points of a spherical mirror is called the operature or linear operature.

concepts.title: Sign Conventions in Mirror
concepts.conceptBody:
Sign Conventions in Mirror 

Although a number of books follow different sign conventions, but we will be using the following sign conventions :

  1. Whenever and wherever possible, the ray of light is taken to travel from left to right.
  2. The distances above principal axis are taken to be positive while below it is negative. 
  3. Along the principal axis, distances are measured from the pole and in the direction of light are taken to be positive while opposite if is negative. This is why in our convention, $u$ (or v) is taken to be -ve if in front of the mirror and +ve if behind it.

concepts.title: Paraxial Rays
concepts.conceptBody:
Paraxial Rays

Rays which are close to the principal axis and make small angles with it, i.e., they are nearly parallel to the axis, are called paraxial rays.

Our treatment of spherical mirrors will be restricted to such rays which means we shall consider only mirrors of small aperture. In diagrams, however, they will be made larger for clarity.

And rays far away from principal axis are called Marginal Rays.

concepts.title: Ray Tracing
concepts.conceptBody:
Ray Tracing

  1. Ray parallel to principal axis after reflection passes through focus.
  2. Rays parallel to each other but not parallel to principal axis form image on the focal plane.
  3. Incident Rays passing through $\text{C}$ retrace their path.
  4. The incident and reflected ray make equal angle with principal axis if the ray is incident at 0 . {this is used to locate the position of mirror}.

concepts.title: Image Formation Rules
concepts.conceptBody:
Image Formation Rules  

1.A ray parallel to principal axis passes (or appear to pass) through focus after reflection. 

2.A ray passing through or directed towards focus after reflection from the spherical mirror becomes parallel to the principal axis.

3. A ray passing through or directed towards the center of curvature, after reflection from the spherical mirror, retraces its path.

Note: Above rule holds, if incident ray is travelling from left to right else directions just reverses.

Distances measured above principal axis are taken to be positive while distances measured below principal axis are taken to be negative.

notations used :

$\text{u}:\to $ Distance of the object from pole of spherical mirror.

$\text{v}:\to $ distance of the image from the pole of the spherical mirror.

$f:\to $ focal length of the spherical mirror.

$\text{R}:\to $ Radius of curvature of the spherical mirror.

Note: $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ All the symbols used are assigned values with proper sign convention. i.e. for concave mirror $\text{f}$ is always negative and so on.

concepts.title: Images Formed by Concave and Convex Mirror
concepts.conceptBody:
Images Formed by Concave and Convex Mirror  

1.For concave mirror 

2.For convex mirror 

  • In case of image formation unless stated object is taken to be real and extended and is represented by an arrow. Also the mirror is of small size but large radius of curvature and only rays near the axis are considered (otherwise the image will be distorted).

concepts.title: Linear Magnification
concepts.conceptBody:
Linear Magnification

For linear objects, the ratio of the image size (I) to the object size (O) is called linear magnification or transverse magnification or lateral magnification. If is generally denoted by $\text{m}$.

$\begin{array}{l} \text{m}=\frac{\text{ Height }\text{ of }\text{ the }\text{ image }}{\text{ Height }\text{ of }\text{ the }\text{ object }}=\frac{\text{I}}{\text{O}} \\ \text{m}=-\frac{\text{v}}{\text{u}}=\frac{\text{f}}{\text{f}-\text{u}}=\frac{\left( \text{f}-\text{v} \right)}{\text{f}} \\ \end{array}$

Note : (a) $m$ will be negative for a real-inverted image of a real object.

(b) $\text{m}$ will be positive for a virtual erect image of a real object.

concepts.title: Longitudinal Magnification
concepts.conceptBody:
Longitudinal Magnification 

When an object is kept parallel to the principle axis, then let ${{\text{u}}_{1}}$, and ${{\text{u}}_{2}}$ be the distance of the end points of the object and let ${{\text{v}}_{1}},{{\text{v}}_{2}}$ be the ends points of the image formed.

Here, $\text{AB}$ is the object and $\text{A'B'}$ is the image formed.

Note: Please observe that $\text{B'}$ will be closer to $\text{AB}$ as compared to $\text{A'}$.

The point can be seen, as image of the objects closer to the mirror are created farther as compared to the images of the objects farther from the mirror.

Here, $\text{ m}=-\frac{\left( {{\text{v}}_{2}}-{{\text{v}}_{1}} \right)}{\left( {{\text{u}}_{2}}-{{\text{u}}_{1}} \right)}$

also, $\text{ m}=-\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{du}}$ {for short objects$\}$

Note: For spherical mirrors $\text{m}=-\frac{\text{v}}{\text{u}}$. Positive value of $\text{m}$ means $\text{v}$ and $\text{u}$ are of opposite sign. So is $\text{u}$ is negative then $\text{v}$ is positive and vice-versa. Thus, if $m=+2$ for a real object, it means image is virtual, erect and two times greater in size. Similarly $m=-\frac{1}{2}$ means image is real, inverted and $\frac{1}{2}$ times in size (that of object).

concepts.conceptBody:
Nature of Image for Concave and Convex Mirror

1.For Concave Mirror

2. For Convex Mirror

concepts.title: Longitudinal Magnification of Small Object
concepts.conceptBody:
Longitudinal Magnification of Small Object

If an object is placed with its length along the principal axis, then so called longitudinal magnification becomes,

${{\text{m}}_{\text{L}}}=\frac{\text{I}}{\text{O}}=-\left( \frac{{{\text{v}}_{2}}-{{\text{v}}_{1}}}{{{\text{u}}_{2}}-{{\text{u}}_{1}}} \right)=-\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{du}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ small }\!\!~\!\!\text{ objects) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

From, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{\text{v}}+\frac{1}{\text{u}}=\frac{1}{\text{f}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ we have

$-{{\text{v}}^{-2}}\text{dv}-{{\text{u}}^{-2}}\text{du}=0$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dy}}=-{{\left( \frac{\text{v}}{\text{u}} \right)}^{2}}$

concepts.title: Speed of Image
concepts.conceptBody:
Speed of Image

If we differentiate the mirror formula

$\frac{1}{\text{v}}+\frac{1}{\text{u}}=\frac{1}{\text{f}}$

with respect to time, we get

$-{{\text{v}}^{-2}}\cdot \frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dt}}-{{\text{u}}^{-2}}\frac{\text{du}}{\text{dt}}=0\text{ (as }\text{ f}=\text{ constant)}$

Or $

Here $\frac{\text{d}u}{\text{dt}}$ is the rate by which $u$ is changing. Or it is the object speed if mirror is stationary. Similarly, $\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dt}}$ is the rate by which $\text{v}$ (distance between image and mirror) is changing. Or it is image speed if mirror is stationary. So if at a known values of $\text{v}$ and $\text{u}$, the object speed is given, we can find the image speed from the above formula.

Let us take an example for a concave mirror.

Suppose the object is moved from infinity towards focus.

As $u$ is decreasing therefore,

$\left( -\frac{du}{dt} \right)=\text{ rate }\text{ of }\text{ decrease }\text{ of }u$ (speed of object)

Therefore, $\left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)=$ rate of increase of $v$ (speed of image)

Further $\text{v}<\text{u}$ (when the object lies between $\infty $ and C)

$\therefore \left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)<\left( -\frac{du}{dt} \right)$

Hence, as the object is moved towards mirror the image (which is real) will recede from the mirror with speed less than the speed of object.

When the object is at $C$, image is also at $C$, i.e., $v=u$ or $\left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)=\left( -\frac{du}{dt} \right)$. Hence, speed of image is equal to the speed of object. When the object lies between $\text{C}$ and $\text{F}.\text{v}>\text{u}$, i.e., image speed is more than the object speed when object comes inside $\text{F}$ image becomes virtual i.e., $\text{u}$ and $\text{f}$ are negative while $\text{v}$ is positive.

 

Hence, $\frac{1}{\text{v}}-\frac{1}{\text{u}}=-\frac{1}{\text{f}}$

or $\frac{1}{u}-\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}$

or $-{{u}^{-2}}\left( \frac{du}{dt} \right)-{{v}^{-2}}\left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)=0$

Now as $\text{u}$ is further decreased, v also decreases to keep $\frac{1}{\text{f}}$ constant. So, $-\frac{\text{du}}{\text{dt}}$ is rate with which object is approaching towards mirror and $\left( -\frac{dv}{dt} \right)$ is rate by which image is approaching towards mirror. Further in this case we know that image is always enlarged or $v>u$. Therefore, image speed is more than the object speed. Thus, we may conclude the above discussion as under :

When an object is moved from $-\propto $ to $\text{F}$, the image (real) moves from $\text{F}$ to $-\propto $ and the when the object is further moved from $\text{F}$ to $\text{P}$ image (now virtual moves from $+\propto $ to $\text{P}$.

Note : when the object is either at center of curvature $\text{C}$ or at pole $\text{P}$ the two speeds are equal. When the object is at pole it can be assumed as if the image is forming by a plane mirror due to the small aperture of the mirror.

concepts.title: Graph Between 1/v versus 1/u
concepts.conceptBody:
Graph Between 1/v versus 1/u

Let us first take the case of a concave mirror. Here, two cases are possible.

 

Case 1. When the image is real, i.e., object lies between $\text{F}$ and infinity. In such a situation $\text{u}$, $\text{v}$ and $\text{f}$ are negative. Hence, the mirror formula

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} & \text{becomes }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ -\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=-\frac{1}{f} & ~ \\ \end{array}$

Or again $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{\text{v}}+\frac{1}{\text{u}}=\frac{1}{\text{f}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{\text{v}}=-\frac{1}{\text{u}}+\frac{1}{\text{f}}$

Comparing with $\text{y}=-\text{x}+\text{c}$, the desired graph will be a straight line with slope -1 and slope equal to $\frac{1}{\text{f}}$.

The corresponding graph is shown in figure

Case 2. When the image is virtual, i.e., object lies between $F$ and $P$. Under such situation $u$ and $\text{f}$ are negative while $\text{v}$ is positive. The mirror formula thus becomes

$\frac{1}{\text{v}}=\frac{1}{\text{u}}-\frac{1}{\text{f}}$

Comparing it with $y=x-c$ the desired graph is a straight line with slope +1 and intercept $-\frac{1}{f}$. The graph is thus shown in Fig. (a) The two graphs can be drawn in one single graph as in Fig. (b)

Note : Here $\frac{1}{\text{u}}$ and $\frac{1}{\text{v}}$ are really the magnitudes of $\frac{1}{\text{u}}$ and $\frac{1}{\text{v}}$ (i.e., without sign) 

For a convex mirror image is always virtual, i.e., $\text{u}$ is negative while $\text{v}$ and $\text{f}$ are positive. Hence, the mirror formula becomes.

$\frac{1}{\text{v}}=\frac{1}{\text{u}}+\frac{1}{\text{f}}$

Comparing with $\text{y}=\text{x}+\text{c}$, the desired graph is a straight line of

slope +1 and intercept $\frac{1}{\text{f}}$.
